title: NodeSplittingHard
tex_block_html:
Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CADMG and $W \ins V$ a subset of the output nodes. The single-world intervention graph (SWIG) w.r.t.\ $W$ of $G$ is the CADMG: \[ G_{\swig(W)} :=\lp J_{\swig(W)}, V_{\swig(W)}, E_{\swig(W)},L_{\swig(W)} \rp,\]

constructed as follows. We first make two disjont copies of the nodes in $W$: \[ W^o:=\lC w^o\st w \in W \rC, \qquad W^i:=\lC w^i \st w \in W \rC. \] Note that we consider $w^o \neq w^i$ for $w \in W$. However, for brevity, for $v \in J \cup V \sm W$ we put: \[ v^o:=v^i:=v. \] We then define:

  1. $J_{\swig(W)} := J \dcup W^i$,
  2. $V_{\swig(W)} := (V \sm W) \dcup W^o$,
  3. $E_{\swig(W)} := \lC v^i_1 \tuh v_2^o \st v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E \rC$,
  4. $L_{\swig(W)} :=\lC v_1^o \huh v_2^o \st v_1 \huh v_2 \in L \rC$.

where we turn all nodes of $W^i$ into input nodes, removing all edges into $W^i$, and we turn all nodes of $W^o$ into output nodes, removing all edges out of $W^o$.

tex_statement.html:
Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CADMG (def def-cdmg-types, item i., equivalently a CDMG of def def-cdmg that is acyclic in the sense of def def-acylic); throughout we use the notation of def not-cdmg and recall the typing and axioms of def def-cdmg, namely $J \cap V = \emptyset$, $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$, $L \subseteq V \times V$, $L$ irreflexive ($(v_1, v_2) \in L \Rightarrow v_1 \neq v_2$), and $L$ symmetric ($(v_1, v_2) \in L \Leftrightarrow (v_2, v_1) \in L$). Let $W \ins V$ be a subset of the output nodes; in particular $W \cap J = \emptyset$, since $W \ins V$ and $J \cap V = \emptyset$, so the construction acts only on output-side nodes.

Paradigm observation (which axioms each clause uses). The CADMG hypothesis on $G$ bundles together the CDMG typing axioms of def def-cdmg ($J \cap V = \emptyset$, $E \ins (J \cup V) \times V$, $L \ins V \times V$, $L$ symmetric and irreflexive) and the acyclicity hypothesis of def def-acylic. Items i.–iv.\ below, and the CDMG axiom-check at the end, in fact only consume the CDMG typing axioms; the acyclicity hypothesis is only consumed downstream, by claim_3_9, which asserts that the constructed $G_{\swig(W)}$ is itself acyclic under the CADMG hypothesis on $G$. This is an observation about which axioms each clause uses; it does not weaken the standing CADMG hypothesis above, which we keep as the literal LN's.

The single-world intervention graph (SWIG) w.r.t.\ $W$ of $G$ is the CADMG \[ G_{\swig(W)} := \lp J_{\swig(W)}, V_{\swig(W)}, E_{\swig(W)}, L_{\swig(W)} \rp, \] constructed as follows.

Conceptual motivation (informal). The LN's commented-out shorthand $G((V \sm W) \dcup W^o \mid \doit(W^i \dcup J))$ encodes the SWIG construction as “node-split (def def:G_node-splitting) at $W$, then hard-intervene (def def:G_hard_intervention) at the input-side copies $W^i$”. Concretely, node-splitting introduces two tagged copies $W^o$ and $W^i$ of $W$ together with transfer edges $w^o \tuh w^i$ for each $w \in W$; hard intervention at $W^i$ then both reclassifies $W^i$ as input nodes and removes every directed edge whose head lies in $W^i$ (in particular, removes the transfer edges). Items i.–iv.\ below realise this composite construction directly as four set-builders, without first constructing the intermediate node-split graph: nothing is “included and then removed”.

Tagged copies of $W$. We introduce two tagged copies of the nodes in $W$, \[ W^o := \lC w^o \st w \in W \rC, \qquad W^i := \lC w^i \st w \in W \rC, \] where $w \mapsto w^o$ and $w \mapsto w^i$ are injective and produce elements that are of a formally distinct kind from every element of $J \cup V$ and from each other: in a formal encoding, $W^o$ and $W^i$ are realised as tagged copies (e.g.\ via a $\Sigma$-type or tagged sum over $W$) so that the disjointness assertions \[ W^o \cap V \;=\; W^i \cap V \;=\; W^o \cap J \;=\; W^i \cap J \;=\; W^o \cap W^i \;=\; \emptyset \] hold automatically by typing rather than being asserted as a side condition; in particular $w^o \neq w^i$ for every $w \in W$, matching the LN's note that “$w^o \neq w^i$ for $w \in W$”.

Notational shorthand for non-$W$ nodes. For $v \in J \cup (V \sm W)$ we put \[ v^o \;:=\; v^i \;:=\; v, \] purely as notational shorthand to be used inside the set-builder definitions of $E_{\swig(W)}$ and $L_{\swig(W)}$ below. This convention does not reassign such $v$ into either tagged-copy carrier $W^o$ or $W^i$: untagged nodes $v \in J \cup (V \sm W)$ remain of their original kind in the ambient carrier, and the writings $v^o$, $v^i$ are simply two labels for $v$ itself. (Note that $J \cup (V \sm W)$ is itself a disjoint union, since $J \cap V = \emptyset$ and $V \sm W \ins V$. The LN writes this set as “$J \cup V \sm W$”; the intended grouping is $J \cup (V \sm W)$, since otherwise the shorthand $v^o := v$ for $v \in W$ would conflict with the tagged construction of $W^o$.)

With these conventions in place, the four components of $G_{\swig(W)}$ are defined as follows.

  1. Input nodes: \[ J_{\swig(W)} := J \dcup W^i, \] understood as a type-level disjoint union per the tagged construction above: each $w^i \in W^i$ is automatically distinct from every element of $J$, so the $\dcup$ holds without a separate side condition.
  2. Output nodes: \[ V_{\swig(W)} := (V \sm W) \dcup W^o, \] understood likewise as a type-level disjoint union: each $w^o \in W^o$ is automatically distinct from every element of $V \sm W$ (and from every element of $J$), so the $\dcup$ holds without a separate side condition.
  3. Directed edges: \[ E_{\swig(W)} \;:=\; \lC (v_1^i, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in E \rC. \] The set-builder ranges over $(v_1, v_2) \in E$, so $v_1 \in J \cup V$ and $v_2 \in V$ by the typing $E \ins (J \cup V) \times V$, and the shorthand $v_1^i, v_2^o$ resolves as follows:
    • if $v_1 \in W$, then $v_1^i$ denotes the tagged copy of $v_1$ in $W^i$; otherwise $v_1 \in J \cup (V \sm W)$ and $v_1^i = v_1$ denotes $v_1$ itself (untagged);
    • if $v_2 \in W$, then $v_2^o$ denotes the tagged copy of $v_2$ in $W^o$; otherwise $v_2 \in V \sm W$ and $v_2^o = v_2$ denotes $v_2$ itself (untagged).

    Equivalently, rendered through def not-cdmg, item 2: every directed edge $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E$ of $G$ is lifted to a directed edge $v_1^i \tuh v_2^o \in E_{\swig(W)}$, and no other directed edges are present in $E_{\swig(W)}$. In particular, every directed edge of $G_{\swig(W)}$ has its source tagged $\cdot^i$ (or in $J \cup (V \sm W)$ under the shorthand) and its target tagged $\cdot^o$ (or in $V \sm W$ under the shorthand).

  4. Bidirected edges: \[ L_{\swig(W)} \;:=\; \lC (v_1^o, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in L \rC. \] The set-builder ranges over $(v_1, v_2) \in L$, so $v_1, v_2 \in V$ by the typing $L \ins V \times V$, and the shorthand $v_k^o$ (for $k \in \{1, 2\}$) resolves as: if $v_k \in W$ then $v_k^o$ denotes the tagged copy of $v_k$ in $W^o$; otherwise $v_k \in V \sm W$ and $v_k^o = v_k$ denotes $v_k$ itself. Equivalently, rendered through def not-cdmg: every bidirected edge $v_1 \huh v_2 \in L$ of $G$ is lifted to a bidirected edge $v_1^o \huh v_2^o \in L_{\swig(W)}$. Note that both endpoints carry the same superscript $^o$; no element of $W^i$ appears as an endpoint of any bidirected edge of $G_{\swig(W)}$. This matches the one-sided $W^0$-only bidirected convention of node-splitting (def def:G_node-splitting, item iv.), and is the reason that bidirected edges incident to $W^o$ survive in $G_{\swig(W)}$ (cf.\ the disambiguation remark on the LN's closing sentence below).

The CDMG axioms (def def-cdmg) hold for $G_{\swig(W)}$: $J_{\swig(W)} \cap V_{\swig(W)} = \emptyset$ (since $J \cap V = \emptyset$, $J \cap W^o = \emptyset$, $W^i \cap (V \sm W) = \emptyset$, and $W^i \cap W^o = \emptyset$, all from the tagged-copy construction); the typing $E_{\swig(W)} \ins (J_{\swig(W)} \cup V_{\swig(W)}) \times V_{\swig(W)}$ holds by case analysis on the set-builder's source $v_1^i$ (if $v_1 \in W$, then $v_1^i \in W^i \ins J_{\swig(W)}$; if $v_1 \in J$, then $v_1^i = v_1 \in J \ins J_{\swig(W)}$; if $v_1 \in V \sm W$, then $v_1^i = v_1 \in V \sm W \ins V_{\swig(W)}$, hence into $J_{\swig(W)} \cup V_{\swig(W)}$ in every case) and on its target $v_2^o$ (if $v_2 \in W$, then $v_2^o \in W^o \ins V_{\swig(W)}$; if $v_2 \in V \sm W$, then $v_2^o = v_2 \in V \sm W \ins V_{\swig(W)}$, hence into $V_{\swig(W)}$ in every case); the typing $L_{\swig(W)} \ins V_{\swig(W)} \times V_{\swig(W)}$ holds analogously, both endpoints $v_1^o$ and $v_2^o$ lying in $W^o \cup (V \sm W) \ins V_{\swig(W)}$ by the same $W$-vs-$V \sm W$ case split; symmetry of $L_{\swig(W)}$ follows from symmetry of $L$ (the set-builder commutes with swapping the two coordinates: $(v_1, v_2) \in L \Leftrightarrow (v_2, v_1) \in L$ lifts to $(v_1^o, v_2^o) \in L_{\swig(W)} \Leftrightarrow (v_2^o, v_1^o) \in L_{\swig(W)}$); and irreflexivity of $L_{\swig(W)}$ follows from irreflexivity of $L$ together with injectivity of $v \mapsto v^o$ on $V$ (so $v_1^o = v_2^o \Rightarrow v_1 = v_2$, contradicting $v_1 \neq v_2$ from $L$'s irreflexivity). Note that this axiom-check establishes only that $G_{\swig(W)}$ is a CDMG; acyclicity of $G_{\swig(W)}$ – the remaining clause needed to upgrade it to a CADMG, matching the LN's claim that the SWIG is itself a CADMG – is not derived from items i.–iv.\ here. It is deferred to claim_3_9, which establishes acyclicity of $G_{\swig(W)}$ under the standing CADMG hypothesis on $G$.

Informally: every directed edge $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E$ of $G$ is reattached as a directed edge from the input-side tag of $v_1$ to the output-side tag of $v_2$, so in $G_{\swig(W)}$ the source slot of every directed edge is $\cdot^i$-tagged (or untagged in $J \cup (V \sm W)$) and the target slot is $\cdot^o$-tagged (or untagged in $V \sm W$); every bidirected edge of $G$ is reattached at the output-side tags of both endpoints. The input-side copies $W^i$ act as input nodes in $G_{\swig(W)}$ – they appear in $J_{\swig(W)}$ and never as an endpoint of any edge of $G_{\swig(W)}$ at either slot – while the output-side copies $W^o$ act as output nodes – they appear in $V_{\swig(W)}$, never as the source of any directed edge, but may appear at either endpoint of a bidirected edge.

Disambiguation of the LN's closing remark. The LN closes the definition with the sentence

“we turn all nodes of $W^i$ into input nodes, removing all edges into $W^i$, and we turn all nodes of $W^o$ into output nodes, removing all edges out of $W^o$.”

This sentence is to be read as a descriptive gloss on items i.–iv., not as a separate edge-deletion step performed after some intermediate graph: nothing is ever included in $E_{\swig(W)}$ or $L_{\swig(W)}$ and then deleted, because items iii.\ and iv.\ build the edge sets directly. With that in mind, the two halves of the gloss are interpreted as follows.

  1. “Removing all edges into $W^i$.” Every directed edge of $G_{\swig(W)}$ has its target tagged $\cdot^o$ (item iii.), so no directed edge has a target in $W^i$; every bidirected edge of $G_{\swig(W)}$ has both endpoints tagged $\cdot^o$ (item iv.), so no bidirected edge touches $W^i$ at either endpoint. Hence “edges into $W^i$” means both directed and bidirected edges, and the construction admits none of either.
  2. “Removing all edges out of $W^o$.” Here the LN's “edges” is to be read as referring to directed edges only: every directed edge of $G_{\swig(W)}$ has its source tagged $\cdot^i$ (item iii.), so no directed edge has a source in $W^o$. Bidirected edges, by contrast, may legitimately have an endpoint in $W^o$: item iv.\ places, for every bidirected edge $w_1 \huh w_2 \in L$ with $w_1, w_2 \in W$, the bidirected edge $w_1^o \huh w_2^o \in L_{\swig(W)}$ with both endpoints in $W^o$. So “edges out of $W^o$” must not be read as “all edges incident to $W^o$”; the LN's sentence is faithful to items iii.–iv.\ only under the “directed-only” reading of “out of”.

Self-loops on $W$ produce no cycles in $G_{\swig(W)}$. The CDMG type of def def-cdmg does not require $E$ to be self-loop-free: the directed-self-loop case $(w, w) \in E$ for $w \in J \cup V$ is admitted. For $w \in W$ with $(w, w) \in E$, item iii.\ produces the lifted edge $(w^i, w^o) \in E_{\swig(W)}$, with $w^i \in W^i \ins J_{\swig(W)}$ and $w^o \in W^o \ins V_{\swig(W)}$; this is not a self-loop, since $w^i \neq w^o$ by the tagged-copy construction. Moreover it does not form a length-$2$ cycle with any other edge in $E_{\swig(W)}$: any other directed edge in $E_{\swig(W)}$ has a $\cdot^i$-tagged (or $J \cup (V \sm W)$) source and a $\cdot^o$-tagged (or $V \sm W$) target by item iii., so in particular no edge in $E_{\swig(W)}$ has $w^o$ as a source. Consequently no cycle in $G_{\swig(W)}$ is produced by a directed self-loop on $W$, in contrast to the node-splitting construction of def def:G_node-splitting, where the same self-loop $(w, w) \in E$ would produce a length-$2$ directed cycle $w^0 \tuh w^1 \tuh w^0$ together with the transfer edge $(w^0, w^1)$; no such transfer edge is included in $E_{\swig(W)}$. (Bidirected self-loops are vacuous in either construction, since $L$ is irreflexive by def def-cdmg.)

lean_blocks.explanation: This is a `variable` declaration: Lean auto-binds `Node : Type*` and the instance `[DecidableEq Node]` as implicit arguments to every subsequent declaration in the surrounding `CDMG` namespace, sparing us from rewriting the same parameters on `nodeSplittingHard`. The `Type*` notation makes `Node` universe-polymorphic, so callers can instantiate it at any universe level. The `DecidableEq Node` assumption is load-bearing for the SWIG construction in particular: the body uses `Finset.image` (for the three set-builder image clauses building $J'$, $V'$, $E'$, $L'$ over the tagged carrier `SplitNode Node`), `Finset.union` (to form the two-piece carriers $J \cup W^i$ and $(V \setminus W) \cup W^o$), and `Finset.sdiff` (for `G.V \ W`) — all of which need decidable equality on the element type to compute the resulting `Finset`. Stronger structure on `Node` (e.g.\ `Fintype`, `LinearOrder`) is not required here and is deferred to downstream consumers that actually use it. Same convention as the rest of `Chapter3_GraphTheory`'s files.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Auto-binds `Node` + `DecidableEq Node` as implicits; decidability drives `Finset.image`/`union`/`sdiff` below.
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.explanation: This is the main declaration of the row: the *single-world intervention graph* (SWIG) operation $G \mapsto G_{\mathrm{swig}(W)}$ on a CADMG, packaged as a Lean `def`. The signature takes a source CDMG `G`, its acyclicity witness `hG : G.IsCADMG`, the intervened-output subset `W : Finset Node`, and the subset-of-output-nodes witness `hW : W ⊆ G.V`, and returns a CDMG on the enlarged carrier `SplitNode Node` introduced by `def_3_11`. Inside the carrier, the LN's $^o$-tag corresponds to the `.copy0` constructor and $^i$ to `.copy1`, while $J \cup (V \setminus W)$ inhabits the `.unsplit` constructor. The four CDMG fields are assigned directly via the LN's item-by-item set-builders, and the four CDMG obligations (`hJV_disj`, `hE_subset`, `hL_subset`, `hL_irrefl`) are discharged by the four `private lemma`s factored above. Both `hG` and `hW` ride on the signature for LN-faithfulness but are not consumed by the field assignments; `set_option linter.unusedVariables false` absorbs them. Acyclicity *preservation* — that $G_{\mathrm{swig}(W)}$ is itself a CADMG — is deferred to a downstream lemma (the `Sym2`-typed analogue of `claim_3_9`).
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- `nodeSplittingHard G hG W hW` is the SWIG of `G` w.r.t. the intervened-output subset `W`.
def nodeSplittingHard (G : CDMG Node)
    -- `hG` is the LN's "Let `G` be a CADMG" precondition; carried for faithfulness, not consumed below.
    (hG : G.IsCADMG) (W : Finset Node) (hW : W ⊆ G.V) :
    -- Enlarged carrier `SplitNode Node` (reused from `def_3_11`): `.copy0 ↔ ^o`, `.copy1 ↔ ^i`, `.unsplit ↔` untagged.
    CDMG (SplitNode Node)

design_choices:
**Shape: a plain `def` returning `CDMG (SplitNode Node)`, not a `class`, a fresh `inductive`, nor a `structure` wrapping the result.** The SWIG is data computed from `(G, hG, W, hW)` — a concrete CDMG on an enlarged carrier — so the natural Lean rendering builds the four `CDMG` fields directly. A `class` is wrong because the SWIG is a parameterised operation, not a property to be resolved by typeclass search; a fresh `inductive SwigGraph` would force a coercion `SwigGraph → CDMG (SplitNode Node)` at every downstream destructure site; and a `structure ... where output : CDMG ...` wrapper would obfuscate that the SWIG is itself a CDMG of the same kind as `G`. The chosen shape stays uniform with the other chapter-3 operators (`hardInterventionOn`, `nodeSplittingOn`), all of which are `CDMG → ⋯ → CDMG` maps at the type level.

**The carrier is `SplitNode Node`, reusing `def_3_11`'s tagged-sum inductive rather than introducing a parallel `SwigNode`.** The LN's `v^o ≠ v^i` for `v ∈ W` combined with `v^o = v^i = v` for `v ∉ W` is precisely the universal property of `SplitNode`'s three constructors (`unsplit`, `copy0`, `copy1`). Reusing it places the SWIG and the node-split graph on the *same* carrier (so any future lemma relating them is a literal equation, not a threaded coercion), reuses the public helpers `toCopy0` and `toCopy1` verbatim with the SWIG-side reading `.copy0 ↔ ^o`, `.copy1 ↔ ^i`, and aligns constructor semantics — `.copy0` was already the 'natural / observational' side in `def_3_11`, matching SWIG's $^o$. Alternatives `Node ⊕ (Node × Bool)`, `Sum Node Node`, and a parallel `inductive SwigNode` were all rejected for the duplication and coercion overhead they would impose.

**`L : Finset (Sym2 (SplitNode Node))`, not `Finset (SplitNode Node × SplitNode Node)`.** Under `cdmg_typed_edges`, `CDMG.L` carries unordered pairs via Mathlib's `Sym2` quotient, so the SWIG's `L` field is built as `G.L.image (Sym2.map (toCopy0 W))`. Three structural simplifications follow at this row. First, no `hL_symm` obligation appears in the output — swap-symmetry `s(v_1, v_2) = s(v_2, v_1)` is definitional on `Sym2`. Second, `hL_subset` is universally quantified via `Sym2.Mem` and discharges in one `Sym2.mem_map` chain rather than a two-coordinate destructure. Third, `hL_irrefl` discharges via `¬ s.IsDiag` through Mathlib's `Sym2.isDiag_map` injectivity lift — a one-line discharge where an ordered-pair `L`-encoding would require a four-step `congrArg Prod.fst/snd` + manual `toCopy0`-injectivity pipeline. The injectivity premise is supplied by the local `swig_toCopy0_inj` helper, mirroring `nodeSplittingOn`'s `toCopy0_inj` idiom.

**Direct four-set-builder construction, not the composition `(G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).hardInterventionOn (W.image .copy1) _`.** The SWIG admits a conceptual reading as 'node-split at $W$, then hard-intervene at $W^i$', and the composed Lean form is type-correct. The direct form is preferred for three reasons: it matches the LN's item-by-item statement, so the website view renders four clauses line by line rather than a composite of two unrelated operators; definitional unfolding for downstream membership queries reduces to one `Finset.mem_image` per field rather than a two-stage chain; and the composed form would include transfer edges `(.copy0 w, .copy1 w)` that the outer hard intervention immediately removes, whereas the direct form simply never includes them, realising the LN's 'nothing is included and then removed' framing literally.

**Acyclicity is handled asymmetrically: `hG : G.IsCADMG` sits on the input signature, but the output type is `CDMG (SplitNode Node)`, not a CADMG.** The LN's 'Let $G$ be a CADMG' is a hypothesis on the input domain, so the binder belongs on the signature; dropping it would silently widen the LN's input quantifier to admit non-acyclic graphs. The four field assignments and the four CDMG obligations genuinely do not consume `hG`, exactly as the LN spec's 'paradigm observation' paragraph asserts. The `set_option linter.unusedVariables false` absorbs the deliberately-unused `hG` and `hW` binders. Acyclicity *preservation* — that $G_{\mathrm{swig}(W)}$ is itself acyclic — is deferred to a downstream `Sym2`-typed analogue of `claim_3_9`, mirroring the chapter-3 pattern at `hardInterventionOn` and `nodeSplittingOn`.

**J and V split two-piece-per-side, departing from `def_3_11`'s three-piece V-carrier.** Item i lands `J' := G.J.image .unsplit ∪ W.image .copy1` (the LN's `W^i` copies are *reclassified as input nodes*), and item ii lands `V' := (G.V \ W).image .unsplit ∪ W.image .copy0`. At the `hJV_disj` level this manifests as a four-way constructor-disjointness case split (`J vs V\W` by `G.hJV_disj`; the three cross-pairs by constructor mismatch). The asymmetry vs. `def_3_11`'s `(V \ W) ∪ W^0 ∪ W^1` on the V-side is the literal SWIG specification.

**Item iii is single-clause (no transfer edges), and item iv lifts both endpoints through `toCopy0`.** Item iii is `G.E.image (fun e => (toCopy1 W e.1, toCopy0 W e.2))` — no transfer summand, contrasting `def_3_11`'s two-clause E that adds `(.copy0 w, .copy1 w)` edges. The omission is forced by the SWIG's 'hard-intervene at $W^i$' semantics: the transfer edge would have its target in $W^i$, exactly what the LN's closing remark removes. Item iv applies `Sym2.map (toCopy0 W)` so both endpoints carry the $^o$ tag — never $^i$ — matching the LN's literal item iv reading. Inverting to `toCopy1` would silently relocate bidirected confounding between intervened nodes onto the input-side copies, breaking downstream `claim_3_22` (σ-separation symmetry) and the do-calculus / SWIG factorisation lemmas.

**The LN's closing 'removing all edges into $W^i$ / out of $W^o$' is realised structurally, not as a deletion step.** Item iii's set-builder `(toCopy1 W e.1, toCopy0 W e.2)` can never produce a `.copy1`-tagged target nor a `.copy0`-tagged source; item iv's `Sym2.map (toCopy0 W)` cannot produce a `.copy1` endpoint at either slot. So no directed edge runs into $W^i$ or out of $W^o$, and no bidirected edge touches $W^i$ at all — not because such edges are filtered out, but because they are never included to begin with. The LN's procedurally-phrased 'removal' translates to its structural referent.

**Field assignment via `where` syntax with named fields, not the anonymous constructor `⟨ … ⟩`.** The `CDMG` structure has eight fields — `hL_symm` is absent because swap-symmetry is definitional on `Sym2`. Named-field `where` keeps every field labelled, lets each proof obligation sit next to the data it constrains, and mirrors `nodeSplittingOn`'s choice verbatim. The four CDMG obligations are factored as four `private lemma`s above the def rather than inlined; this isolates each LN-clause obligation behind a focused error message, keeps the def body pure data + lemma references for the website's rendered signature, and exposes each per-obligation invariant as a citable top-level lemma should a downstream consumer need it.

**Argument order `(G, hG, W, hW)`.** `hG` sits between `G` and `W` because it is a side condition on `G` itself; this mirrors `nodeSplittingOn`'s `(G, W, hW)` order with the addition of the LN-CADMG hypothesis, and keeps the LN-faithful reading 'Let `G` be a CADMG; let `W ⊆ V`' visible on the signature.
